content.text: What it is
A
Prioritization Committee Process

is a structured governance mechanism by which a cross-functional committee of senior stakeholders evaluates, scores, and approves or rejects AI project proposals on a regular cadence. Rather than letting individual teams self-authorize new initiatives — or routing every request through a single executive bottleneck — the committee distributes accountability across the people who actually bear the consequences: product leaders who will own the roadmap, engineering leads who will staff the work, finance who controls the budget, and risk or legal who must sign off on compliance exposure.
The committee assigns every proposal a tier classification:
Must-Do
(regulatory or contractual obligation),
Should-Do
(clear strategic value),
Nice-to-Have
(backlog for a future cycle), or
Rejected

(not approved, with written rationale). Decisions are recorded, communicated, and tracked in a shared system of record — typically GitHub Issues linked to a Confluence page — so there is no ambiguity about what was decided, by whom, and why.
Why it matters

Enterprise AI portfolios are expensive to staff and slow to course-correct. Without a formal intake and prioritization gate, organisations accumulate the pathologies typical of uncoordinated growth: teams build overlapping capabilities, critical dependencies are missed until late in delivery, regulatory obligations are discovered in QA rather than at intake, and executives find it impossible to give a coherent answer to the board about what the company is building and why.

A committee process solves the coordination problem by creating a single moment of deliberate, cross-functional alignment before any significant resource commitment is made. Because decisions happen in a structured forum with a pre-read packet distributed in advance, participants arrive informed rather than reactive. Because quorum rules prevent a single function from dominating, the outcome reflects genuine organisational consensus rather than the loudest voice in the room.

For AI specifically, the committee structure matters more than in traditional software because AI projects carry unique risks — data lineage questions, model bias surface area, third-party API cost volatility, regulatory ambiguity around AI Act or NIST AI RMF — that engineering and product alone are not equipped to evaluate. Bringing risk and legal in at intake rather than post-build avoids the expensive rework of rebuilding a system that could not actually be deployed.
Benefits
Cross-functional alignment before commitment:
product, engineering, finance, and risk sign off together, so no single stakeholder can unilaterally block or green-light a project after the fact.
Explicit tier classification:
the must-do / should-do / nice-to-have / rejected taxonomy gives execution teams a clear signal about urgency and resource priority without ambiguous verbal commitments.
Regulatory risk surfaced early:
legal and risk representatives see the proposal before any code is written, converting compliance blockers into intake criteria rather than post-build surprises.
Portfolio-level visibility:
the committee sees all competing proposals in the same session, making trade-offs between initiatives explicit rather than implicit.
Audit trail:

GitHub Issues and Confluence pages provide a permanent record of every decision, the score it received, and the rationale — essential for regulatory audits and for evaluating decision quality over time.
Predictable cadence:
monthly intake meetings and quarterly portfolio rebalancing give teams a reliable target date for submitting proposals and a known timeline for receiving decisions.
Trade-offs
Latency:

a monthly cadence means the fastest a proposal can move from submission to approved is 30 days. Truly urgent work (production incident response, regulatory emergency) requires an expedited track that bypasses the standard cycle.
Bureaucratic overhead:
preparing a complete pre-read packet — executive summary, scoring card, dependency map, resource ask — takes submitters 4–8 hours. Low-effort speculative ideas may not justify the submission cost.
Quorum fragility:
if the committee has too many required members, one unavoidable absence delays decisions. Calibrate quorum to 60–70% of voting members rather than 100%.
Risk of groupthink:
a committee dominated by one function (e.g., product) will systematically under-weight infrastructure or security concerns. Explicit seat allocation and rotating facilitation reduce this.
Score gaming:

once submitters learn the scoring rubric, they can inflate scores by framing every proposal as strategic or regulatory. Calibration sessions where the committee reviews past scores against actual outcomes help maintain rubric integrity.
How it works
The process runs as a repeating cycle with five phases:
Proposal Submission (weeks 1–2 of the cycle):

any team can submit a proposal via the intake form. The form collects the business problem, proposed AI solution, expected ROI, data requirements, model risk classification, dependency list, and a resource ask broken down by role and duration. Incomplete submissions are returned to the submitter before they enter the committee queue.
Pre-Read Preparation (week 3):

the programme management office (PMO) or AI governance team aggregates submitted proposals into a single pre-read packet. Each proposal gets a one-page executive summary, a populated scoring card (impact, feasibility, risk, strategic alignment on a 1–5 scale), and a dependency map showing intersections with existing projects. The packet is distributed to committee members at least five business days before the meeting.
Committee Review Meeting (week 4):

a two-to-three-hour monthly session. Each proposal gets a fixed time slot (typically 10–15 minutes for standard proposals, 20–30 minutes for high-complexity or high-risk ones). The submitter presents for five minutes; the committee discusses; a vote is called. The sponsor (CTO or CDO) chairs; the PMO takes verbatim decision notes.
Decision and Communication (within 48 hours):

the PMO publishes the decision record to Confluence and creates a GitHub Issue per approved project. Submitters of rejected proposals receive a written rationale within 48 hours. All-hands or department Slack channels are updated with a brief summary of approvals.
Execution and Quarterly Rebalancing:

approved projects enter the delivery pipeline. At the quarterly rebalancing meeting, the committee reviews the full portfolio — pausing or cancelling projects that have missed milestones or whose business case has weakened, and pulling nice-to-have items off the backlog if capacity opens up.
Architecture
The diagram below shows the end-to-end committee process flow from proposal submission through decision classification to execution.

attrs.alt: Prioritization Committee Process Flow: Proposal Submission → Pre-Read Preparation → Committee Review Meeting → Decision (Must-Do / Should-Do / Nice-to-Have / Rejected) → Communication → Execution

content.text: The
must-do
track fast-paths to immediate resource allocation with executive oversight.
Should-do
items slot into the next quarterly roadmap cycle.
Nice-to-have
items park in a prioritised backlog and are reconsidered at each quarterly rebalancing.
Rejected
proposals receive a written rationale and may be revised and resubmitted in a future cycle.
When to use it
Portfolio scale reaches 8+ concurrent AI initiatives:
below this threshold, informal alignment is workable. Above it, dependency collisions and resource conflicts become common enough to justify the overhead of a formal committee.
Regulatory exposure is material:
any organisation operating under GDPR, HIPAA, financial services regulation, or the EU AI Act needs a documented intake process that demonstrates proportionate risk assessment before AI deployment.
Multiple business units are competing for shared AI infrastructure:
a central GPU cluster, a shared vector database, or a fine-tuned foundation model is a constrained resource. Without a committee, allocation defaults to whoever shouts loudest.
Executive trust in AI delivery is low:
after failed or overrunning projects, boards and C-suites want visible governance. A committee with a documented process and a public decision log rebuilds trust by making accountability legible.
Mergers or acquisitions have created competing AI roadmaps:
post-acquisition integration produces duplicate initiatives across legacy organisations. A committee process is the cleanest way to rationalise them.
How to implement
Below is a YAML template covering the meeting agenda and the decision record format. Commit one decision record per meeting to your governance repository.

# ai-governance/committee/2024-Q2-intake-meeting.yaml

meeting:
  date: 2024-04-15
  cadence: monthly-intake
  chair: CDO          # rotating co-chair from product side
  facilitator: PMO-lead
  quorum_required: 7  # of 12 voting members
  quorum_achieved: 9
  attendees:
    - role: CTO
      name: Alex Chen
      voting: true
    - role: CDO
      name: Priya Patel
      voting: true
    - role: VP Product — Commerce
      name: Jordan Lee
      voting: true
    - role: VP Product — Fulfilment
      name: Sam Torres
      voting: true
    - role: VP Engineering — Platform
      name: Dana Kim
      voting: true
    - role: VP Engineering — Data
      name: Morgan Reyes
      voting: true
    - role: Head of Finance — Technology
      name: Riley Nguyen
      voting: true
    - role: Head of Risk
      name: Casey Park
      voting: true
    - role: Senior Counsel — Technology
      name: Blake Johnson
      voting: true
    - role: Head of AI Engineering
      name: Avery Williams
      voting: false  # subject-matter adviser, non-voting
    - role: PMO Lead
      name: Skyler Brown
      voting: false  # facilitator / scribe

agenda:
  - item: Prior decisions review
    duration_minutes: 10
  - item: Proposal review block
    duration_minutes: 120
    proposals:
      - id: PROP-2024-041
        title: AI-Powered Returns Fraud Detection
        submitter: VP Product Commerce
        time_slot_minutes: 15
      - id: PROP-2024-042
        title: Supplier Demand Forecasting v2
        submitter: VP Engineering Data
        time_slot_minutes: 20
      - id: PROP-2024-043
        title: Customer Sentiment Dashboard (LLM)
        submitter: VP Product Fulfilment
        time_slot_minutes: 10
  - item: Portfolio health check
    duration_minutes: 20
  - item: Next cycle logistics
    duration_minutes: 10

decision_records:
  - proposal_id: PROP-2024-041
    title: AI-Powered Returns Fraud Detection
    scores:
      strategic_alignment: 5
      business_impact: 5
      technical_feasibility: 4
      risk_profile: 3        # PII data handling, model bias risk
      resource_availability: 4
      total_weighted: 4.3
    classification: must-do  # regulatory pressure from payment processor
    rationale: >
      Returns fraud losses exceeded $4.2M in Q1. Payment processor audit
      requires demonstrable fraud controls by 2024-09-01. Risk of contract
      termination classifies this as a regulatory must-do.
    resource_approved:
      engineers: 3
      ml_engineers: 2
      duration_weeks: 16
      gpu_compute: true
    owner: VP Engineering Platform
    target_start: 2024-05-01
    review_checkpoint: 2024-07-01
    decision_date: 2024-04-15
    decided_by: [CTO, CDO, VP-Product-Commerce, VP-Eng-Platform, Head-Risk]
    vote: "9-0 (quorum met)"

  - proposal_id: PROP-2024-042
    title: Supplier Demand Forecasting v2
    scores:
      strategic_alignment: 4
      business_impact: 4
      technical_feasibility: 3
      risk_profile: 4
      resource_availability: 2  # platform team fully booked until Q3
    classification: should-do
    rationale: >
      Strong ROI case ($1.8M inventory reduction). Blocked by platform team
      capacity until Q3. Assigned to Q3 roadmap slot with confirmed resourcing.
    resource_approved:
      engineers: 2
      ml_engineers: 1
      duration_weeks: 12
      gpu_compute: false
    owner: VP Engineering Data
    target_start: 2024-07-15
    review_checkpoint: 2024-09-15
    decision_date: 2024-04-15
    vote: "8-1 (one abstention: resource conflict)"

  - proposal_id: PROP-2024-043
    title: Customer Sentiment Dashboard (LLM)
    scores:
      strategic_alignment: 3
      business_impact: 2
      technical_feasibility: 4
      risk_profile: 4
      resource_availability: 3
    classification: nice-to-have
    rationale: >
      Useful capability but no clear OKR linkage this half. Existing NPS
      tooling partially covers the use case. Parked in backlog; submitter
      encouraged to quantify incremental value over current tooling and
      resubmit in Q3 intake cycle.
    owner: PMO Lead  # backlog holder
    decision_date: 2024-04-15
    vote: "7-2"

next_steps:
  - action: Publish decision record to Confluence
    owner: PMO-lead
    due: 2024-04-17
  - action: Create GitHub Issues for PROP-2024-041, PROP-2024-042
    owner: PMO-lead
    due: 2024-04-17
  - action: Send rejection letter to PROP-2024-043 submitter
    owner: PMO-lead
    due: 2024-04-17
  - action: Kick-off for PROP-2024-041 fraud detection project
    owner: VP Engineering Platform
    due: 2024-05-01
Examples
The following scenarios illustrate how the tier classification system handles common enterprise situations:
Must-Do — GDPR Right-to-Explanation:

the legal team notifies the committee that a new AI credit-scoring model requires a documented explanation mechanism under Article 22. The committee classifies this as must-do; engineering lead is asked to scope remediation. Resource is allocated from the current sprint budget rather than waiting for the next quarterly cycle.
Should-Do — Conversational Product Search:

a product VP proposes replacing keyword search with a vector-based semantic search using an LLM query rewriter. Scoring: strategic alignment 5, impact 4, feasibility 3 (new capability requires embedding pipeline), risk 4, resource 3. Total 3.9. Classified should-do; slotted into Q3 roadmap alongside a data engineering prerequisite.
Nice-to-Have — Internal Meeting Summariser:

an engineering manager proposes deploying a Whisper-based meeting transcription and summarisation tool. Useful but no direct revenue link; privacy review needed; similar tools available via existing Microsoft 365 licence. Parked. Submitter directed to first check whether the M365 tooling meets the need.
Rejected — Autonomous Contract Negotiation Agent:

legal counsel flags that autonomous contract execution would require board sign-off on liability limits and potentially new insurance coverage. The committee rejects the proposal as submitted, asking the team to return with a human-in-the-loop design and a legal opinion on liability before resubmitting.
Real-world example

A mid-market UK retail company with 3,400 employees and an annual technology budget of £28M stood up a formal AI Prioritization Committee after two high-profile project failures in 2022: a demand-forecasting model that shipped nine months late and a customer-personalisation engine that had to be pulled three weeks after launch due to an undiscovered GDPR exposure.

The committee was chartered with twelve voting members: the CTO (chair), CDO (co-chair), four product VPs, two engineering VPs, the head of finance for technology, the head of risk, senior technology counsel, and the chief data officer of the retail division. The PMO lead served as facilitator and scribe, non-voting.

Operating cadence: monthly intake meetings on the third Monday of each month (two to three hours), and a half-day quarterly portfolio rebalancing in the final week of each quarter. Quorum was set at seven of twelve voting members. Tie-breaking went to the CTO's casting vote.
In the first full year of operation the committee processed
68 proposals

across four quarterly cycles — an average of 17 per quarter. Classification outcomes: 11 must-do, 24 should-do, 19 nice-to-have, 14 rejected. Of the 35 approved projects (must-do + should-do), 31 delivered to the original scope and timeline — an 89% delivery rate, compared to 54% in the two years prior to the committee.
The company measured
committee member satisfaction

via a quarterly pulse survey. After the first year, 94% of members rated the committee process as effective or very effective. The three specific improvements most cited: pre-read packets distributed five days in advance ("we arrive informed, not reactive"), the five-day written rationale SLA for rejections ("submitters stop lobbying because they know why"), and the GitHub Issue integration that made approved projects visible to all engineering teams.

The GDPR exposure that caused the 2022 pull-down was the catalyst for adding legal counsel as a permanent voting seat rather than an ad hoc attendee. In the committee's first year, legal counsel flagged data-handling issues in five proposals at intake — issues that in prior years would have surfaced in QA or post-launch.
Common mistakes
No pre-read discipline:

distributing the proposal pack 24 hours before the meeting forces participants to read on the spot, turning review into first exposure rather than deliberation. Five business days is the minimum; two weeks is better for complex proposals.
Classifying everything as must-do:

if every proposal is urgent, nothing is. Teams learn quickly that framing a project as regulatory or compliance-critical guarantees approval. Require explicit evidence — a regulatory citation, a contract clause, a legal opinion — to qualify for the must-do tier.
Ignoring the quarterly rebalancing:

monthly intake without quarterly portfolio review accumulates zombie projects — approved but stalled — that consume planning bandwidth and create false impressions of capacity. Rebalancing must have teeth: projects that miss two consecutive milestones are automatically flagged for cancellation or descoping.
Under-specifying quorum and tie-breaking:

if quorum rules are ambiguous, a determined minority can block meetings by staying absent. Spell out: what constitutes a quorum, whether proxy votes are allowed, who holds the casting vote on a tie, and what happens if the casting vote holder is absent.
Decision records only in meeting notes:

meeting notes are ephemeral and hard to query. Decision records must be in a structured, versioned system (GitHub Issues or a dedicated governance database) with consistent metadata — proposal ID, classification, vote, rationale, owner, dates — so they are findable during audits.
No expedited track:

a monthly cadence cannot accommodate a security incident response or a regulatory deadline that is six days away. Define explicit criteria for an expedited review (async vote, 48-hour window, minimum five voting members, sponsor sign-off) and publish the process so teams do not route around the committee entirely for urgent items.
Best practices
Fix committee composition by seat, not by name:

the CTO seat votes, not "whoever the CTO sends." When the seat-holder is absent, a formally designated alternate casts the vote. This prevents attendance creep and ensures accountability stays with the right level of seniority.
Publish the scoring rubric and calibrate annually:

distribute the scoring criteria to all potential submitters. Once a year, run a calibration session where the committee rescores three past proposals against their actual outcomes. Adjust the rubric if the scores systematically mispredicted delivery success or strategic value.
Time-box every proposal slot:

with 15–20 proposals per quarter, a two-hour meeting requires strict time discipline. The facilitator enforces the time box; the chair calls the vote when the box expires. Overrunning one proposal punishes later ones.
Separate the RACI for submission from the RACI for the committee:

the submitter is Responsible for the intake form; the PMO is Accountable for completeness and packet preparation; the committee is Accountable for the decision; the approved project owner is Responsible for execution and milestone reporting back to the committee.
Close the feedback loop on rejections:

a rejection without a specific, actionable rationale demoralises submitters and suppresses future intake. The written rationale must say exactly what the submitter would need to change for the proposal to clear the should-do bar.
Track leading indicators, not just delivery rate:

measure proposal submission volume by business unit (low volume may indicate the process is too burdensome), average time from submission to decision, the ratio of approved projects that hit their first milestone, and committee attendance. These surface process problems before they become delivery problems.
Performance & scalability

The committee process itself does not scale linearly with proposal volume. A two-to-three-hour monthly meeting can process roughly 15–20 proposals with adequate deliberation time. If proposal volume exceeds this, the options are:
Pre-screening filter:
a lightweight PMO filter rejects obviously incomplete or out-of-scope submissions before they reach the committee agenda. This can keep 10–15% of proposals from consuming committee time.
Tiered committees:

large organisations (500+ person engineering organisations) run domain-level sub-committees — one for customer-facing AI, one for internal tooling, one for infrastructure — each with authority to approve should-do and nice-to-have proposals within their domain. The central committee retains authority over must-do and cross-domain proposals.
Consent voting for low-risk proposals:

proposals below a risk and resource threshold (e.g., under £100K and risk score ≤ 3) use an async consent vote — committee members have five business days to object; no objection equals approval. This reserves meeting time for genuinely contested proposals.
Automated scoring pre-population:

the intake form can auto-calculate a preliminary score from structured fields (expected ARR impact, headcount ask, data classification, model risk tier). The committee reviews the auto-score for alignment rather than building the score from scratch. This cuts per-proposal deliberation time from 15 to 8 minutes on average.

Decision latency should be a monitored SLA. Track mean time from submission-close to decision-communicated. A healthy committee processes proposals in under 35 days end-to-end (submission to decision communication). Above 45 days is a signal the process is too slow to be useful for the business.
Tooling & ecosystem
GitHub Issues + Projects:

one issue per approved proposal, tagged with the classification label (must-do, should-do, nice-to-have), assigned to the project owner, and linked to the milestone. The GitHub Projects board gives the committee a real-time portfolio view at quarterly rebalancing. Decision records commit as YAML files to a governance repository.
Confluence:

the canonical human-readable home for committee charters, the scoring rubric, meeting minutes, and the full archive of decision records. Each meeting gets a Confluence page linked from the YAML decision record in Git. The Confluence space is read-accessible to all employees and write-accessible only to the PMO.
Jira:

for organisations already running Jira for project delivery, the approved GitHub Issue is mirrored as a Jira Epic at kick-off. Delivery milestone updates flow from Jira back into the committee's quarterly rebalancing dashboard via a webhook or Jira Automation rule.
Google Forms / Microsoft Forms / Typeform:

the intake form is often the highest-friction point. A structured form that populates a spreadsheet or directly creates a GitHub Issue via an API webhook reduces PMO manual entry. Field validation (mandatory data classification, mandatory resource breakdown) enforces completeness at submission time.
Notion / Linear:

product-led organisations already using Notion for roadmapping can embed the governance process as a Notion database with proposal status properties and linked decision records. Linear works similarly for engineering-led teams.
Slack / Teams integration:

a GitHub Action or Zapier workflow posts decision summaries to a #ai-governance channel immediately after the PMO publishes the decision record. Submitters receive a direct message with their proposal's classification and rationale rather than discovering the outcome days later.
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
How decisions are made
Strengths
Weaknesses
Best fit
Prioritization Committee (this topic)
Cross-functional vote with structured scoring, fixed cadence
High alignment; audit trail; risk surfaced early
Monthly latency; overhead of pre-read prep
8+ concurrent AI initiatives, regulated industries
Executive Sponsor Approval
Single CTO or CDO sign-off
Fast; simple; clear ownership
Bottleneck; no cross-functional buy-in; bias toward sponsor's domain
Organisations under 50 engineers or in rapid startup phase
Team Self-Prioritization (OKR-linked)
Teams choose what to build within their OKR mandate
Fast; high team autonomy; decentralised
No cross-team dependency management; compliance exposure; duplicate work
Small AI teams with no shared infrastructure or regulatory exposure
Investment Committee (VC-style)
Formal pitch process, business case ROI required for all proposals
Rigorous ROI discipline; aligned with finance
Over-indexes on quantifiable ROI; kills exploratory and platform work
CFO-driven organisations; capital allocation decisions above £1M
Async RFC Process
Written RFC circulated for comment; approval by silence or maintainer merge
Good paper trail; asynchronous; no meeting required
Low participation in practice; no cross-functional accountability; scores not calibrated
Technical architecture decisions; open-source style governance
Portfolio Kanban (continuous flow)
Work items flow into a priority queue continuously; top-of-queue is always worked next
No batch latency; naturally limits WIP
Requires disciplined queue management; no structured risk or compliance check
Mature Lean/SAFe organisations with strong PMO discipline
Security & reliability
Access control on decision records:

the governance repository containing YAML decision records should be readable by all employees but writable only by the PMO and via an automated pipeline. Proposals contain commercially sensitive information (budget, ROI projections) and should not be open to public GitHub repositories.
Conflict of interest declaration:

committee members must declare conflicts — a VP whose team submitted a proposal should abstain from voting on that proposal. The decision record must note any abstentions and the reason, creating a defendable audit trail if a decision is later challenged.
Quorum integrity:

record attendance and vote counts, not just the final decision. An internal audit function should be able to verify that quorum was met for every decision. A committee meeting that proceeded without quorum produces decisions that can be challenged.
Decision record immutability:

once a YAML decision record is merged to the main branch of the governance repository, it should require a pull request with at least two PMO approvals to modify. This prevents post-hoc alteration of decisions to align with later outcomes — a risk in any governance process where accountability is at stake.
Sensitivity classification of proposals:

proposals that involve PII, proprietary model weights, or third-party API keys should be stored in a separate restricted Confluence space rather than the general governance space. Reference the proposal ID in the public decision record rather than embedding sensitive details.
Business continuity for the committee itself:

document what happens if the chair is unexpectedly unavailable. Who convenes the meeting? Who holds the casting vote? The committee charter must name alternates for the chair, co-chair, and each voting seat so a single departure cannot incapacitate the process.
Mental model
Think of the Prioritization Committee as a
planning court

— a regularly convened body with defined jurisdiction, standing membership, rules of procedure, and the authority to make binding decisions. Just as a planning court handles applications in batches on a fixed schedule rather than ruling ad hoc, the committee processes proposals in cycles rather than on-demand.
The analogy clarifies three important properties. First,
standing membership matters

: a court that changes its judges every session produces inconsistent rulings. A committee that rotates all its members quarterly loses institutional memory of the scoring rubric and prior decisions. Continuity of at least 60% of voting members across cycles is important.
Second,
jurisdiction must be explicit

: a planning court only rules on planning applications, not on contract disputes. The AI committee's charter must clearly define what kinds of work require committee approval (new AI capabilities above a budget or risk threshold) and what can proceed without it (improvements to existing approved capabilities below a defined materiality threshold).
Third,
the process is the product

: the committee's value is not just the individual decisions it makes but the shared understanding of organisational priorities that the process builds across all twelve members. A product VP who sits in 12 monthly meetings understands the engineering capacity and risk appetite of the organisation in a way that no document or dashboard can replicate. This shared context is what makes subsequent day-to-day alignment faster and cheaper.
Key takeaways

A Prioritization Committee is a cross-functional governance body — CTO/CDO, product VPs, engineering leads, finance, risk, and legal — that meets on a fixed cadence to evaluate AI project proposals against a shared scoring rubric and classify them as must-do, should-do, nice-to-have, or rejected.

The pre-read packet (executive summary, scoring card, dependency map, resource ask) distributed at least five business days before the meeting is what separates deliberation from first exposure; without it, committee time collapses into briefing rather than decision-making.

Quorum rules, tie-breaking mechanisms, conflict-of-interest declarations, and named seat alternates must all be specified in the committee charter before the first meeting — ambiguity in any of these erodes the legitimacy of decisions.

Decision records stored as YAML in a versioned governance repository, linked to Confluence pages and GitHub Issues, provide the audit trail that regulators, boards, and internal audit functions require and that informal meeting notes cannot supply.

The must-do tier requires explicit evidence — a regulatory citation, a contract clause, a legal opinion — to prevent teams from framing every proposal as urgent and collapsing the classification system.

Quarterly portfolio rebalancing with teeth — automatic flagging of stalled projects for cancellation or descoping — prevents the accumulation of zombie approvals that consume planning bandwidth without producing value.

At scale, the process stays workable through pre-screening filters, domain sub-committees, async consent voting for low-risk proposals, and automated score pre-population from the intake form — preserving meeting time for genuinely contested decisions.
